file_name: Ghazal et al. - 2022 - Detection of benign and malignant tumors in skin empowered with transfer learning.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a customized pretrained Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) based on the AlexNet architecture with transfer learning to classify benign and malignant skin tumors. The model was trained on a dataset of 1920 images and achieved 87.1% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a customized pretrained Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) based on AlexNet for classifying benign and malignant skin tumors. The paper includes specific sections on material and methods, experimental setup, results (including accuracy metrics like 87.1%), and a comparison with existing systems, which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: Detection of Benign and Malignant Tumors in Skin Empowered with Transfer Learning
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of benign and malignant skin tumors using a customized pretrained Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) based on the AlexNet architecture. The model was trained on a dataset of 1920 images sourced from Kaggle, with 960 images per class, and validated on 480 images. The authors modified the final layers of AlexNet to perform binary classification, utilizing transfer learning to extract features without handcrafted inputs. Performance was evaluated using metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and likelihood ratios. The proposed system achieved an accuracy of 87.1% on the validation set, outperforming several traditional and existing deep learning methods cited in the literature.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classes in the Kaggle dataset used for training (960 images) and validation (240 images). The paper notes that malignant tumors grow and spread uncontrollably.
One of the two binary classes in the Kaggle dataset used for training (960 images) and validation (240 images). The paper notes that benign tumors remain where they appear and do not spread.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin tumors into benign or malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the problem is to 'classify benign and malignant tumors' and 'tackle the classification problem of skin disease'. The model uses a customized AlexNet with a modified softmax layer for 'binary classification detection'. The output is a discrete diagnostic category (benign vs. malignant), evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and confusion matrix metrics.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Tumor Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available image-based dataset sourced from the Kaggle repository. The original dataset contained 2637 images with imbalanced class sizes. The authors selected a subset of 2400 images for this study, balancing the classes to 1200 images each. The images were resized to 227x227 pixels and converted to RGB format to meet AlexNet input requirements.

use.training: The model was trained on 1920 images (80% of the selected dataset) using different epoch values (10, 20, 30) and a fixed learning rate of 0.001.
use.validation: The model was validated on the remaining 480 images (20% of the selected dataset). The reported accuracy of 87.1% was achieved on this validation set.
use.other: The original dataset (2637 images) was used to derive the subset; no other external datasets were used for training or testing in this specific study, though future work mentions Ph2, MED-NODE, DermIS & DermQuest, ISIC 2017, ISIC 2019, and ISIC 2020.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and confusion matrix values.

Kaggle Skin Tumor Dataset.notes: The model achieved the highest accuracy of 87.1% at 20 epochs. The benign class achieved 93.2% accuracy, while the malignant class achieved 82.5% accuracy. The learning rate was fixed at 0.001. The proposed system outperformed existing systems listed in Table 5 (e.g., SVM [12] at 86.5%, CNN [15] at 83.83%, CNN [30] at 80%, SVM [20] at 81%, Random Forest [27] at 81.46%, CNN [28] at 78%, CNN [29] at 85.62%).

architecture_used.main_model: Customized Pretrained AlexNet (Deep Convolutional Neural Network)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained on ImageNet (over a million images, 1000 object categories)

architecture_details.backbone_structure: 5 convolutional layers, 5 pooling layers, 3 fully connected layers (modified)
architecture_details.modified_layers: Last three layers (fully connected layers and softmax layer) customized for binary classification

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named (standard for pretrained AlexNet in MATLAB)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Customized Pretrained AlexNet (Deep Convolutional Neural Network)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 227x227 pixels to meet AlexNet input requirements
Converting images to RGB color format
Balancing the dataset by selecting 2400 images (1200 per class) from the original imbalanced Kaggle dataset of 2637 images
Splitting the dataset into training (80%, 1920 images) and validation (20%, 480 images) sets
conclusions: The proposed customized pretrained Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) based on AlexNet with transfer learning effectively classifies benign and malignant skin tumors.

The model achieved a maximum accuracy of 87.1% on the validation set (480 images) when trained for 20 epochs, outperforming several existing traditional and deep learning methods cited in the literature (e.g., SVM at 86.5%, CNN at 83.83%, and CNN at 80%).
The system demonstrated high specificity (94.2%) and Positive Predictive Value (93.2%), indicating strong performance in correctly identifying benign cases and minimizing false positives.
The malignant class achieved an accuracy of 82.5% (sensitivity of 80.0%), while the benign class achieved 93.2% accuracy, showing a disparity in performance between the two classes.
Transfer learning allows the model to extract features without relying on handcrafted inputs, addressing challenges such as varying image shapes, sizes, noise, and irregular tumor boundaries.

The study acknowledges that while the proposed system is effective, the detection of malignant tumors remains challenging due to similarities with neighboring tumors and irregular boundaries, suggesting that further exploration of the pretrained network and improvement of malignant class accuracy are areas for future work.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single, publicly available Kaggle dataset (subset of 2400 images) without external validation, limiting generalizability to diverse clinical populations.
The dataset was manually balanced by selecting 1200 images per class from an original imbalanced set of 2637, which may introduce selection bias not present in real-world clinical distributions.
No data augmentation techniques were employed; the model was trained on a fixed set of 1920 images, potentially limiting robustness to variations in image quality and tumor morphology.

The model achieved higher accuracy for benign tumors (93.2%) compared to malignant tumors (82.5%), indicating a potential bias or difficulty in detecting malignant cases, which is clinically critical.
Performance was evaluated only on a validation set (480 images) with no independent test set or cross-validation reported, raising concerns about overfitting and reliability estimation.

The study acknowledges that detecting malignant tumors remains challenging due to similarities with neighboring tumors and irregular boundaries, suggesting current performance may be suboptimal for high-stakes diagnosis.
The system was implemented using MATLAB 2020a on a single CPU, which does not reflect the computational infrastructure or deployment constraints of typical clinical environments.

The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the comparison with existing methods, making it difficult to assess if the 87.1% accuracy is statistically superior to cited baselines.

The authors note that future work will involve fine-tuning all convolutional layers and using larger, well-known datasets (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000), implying the current model's architecture and training protocol are preliminary.
